extra.reasoning: rec_drug_a is set true ONLY by r1, whose condition is (bradycardia and peptic_ulcer and polypharmacy and elderly and low_ejection_fraction). rec_drug_b is set true ONLY by r5, whose condition is just (bradycardia). No rule ever sets these outputs false (defaults apply only when the conditions fail), and priorities are irrelevant because the two outputs are governed by disjoint, non-competing rules. Critically, r1's trigger condition is a strict subset that includes bradycardia, which is exactly r5's trigger. Therefore any input that satisfies r1 necessarily also satisfies r5. With bradycardia=peptic_ulcer=polypharmacy=elderly=low_ejection_fraction=true: r1 fires -> rec_drug_a=true, and r5 fires (bradycardia true) -> rec_drug_b=true. Both are jointly true, violating the mutual-exclusion property NOT(rec_drug_a and rec_drug_b). The other three inputs are don't-cares (set false here); they only affect rec_drug_c, which is irrelevant to the property.
